active-with-message: Active charms should carry an empty status message. A status message on an active unit signals either operational noise that operators must scroll past, or that the charm author intended a different status state. Audit the charm's status-set calls and reserve non-empty messages for non-active states.
blocked-no-message: Blocked means an operator must intervene, but this unit gave no clue what to do. The §4c.2 status protocol requires that 'blocked' carry an actionable message naming the unmet precondition: a missing config key, an unsatisfied relation, an exhausted resource. Audit every status-set call on the blocked path and ensure each names what the operator must change. An empty or whitespace-only message wastes the very signal that justifies the blocked state.
charm-revision-aging: Operators have stewardship over charm revisions. The tracked channel offers a newer revision that brings bug fixes, security patches, or new features. Schedule a refresh window and run 'juju refresh <app>' to pull the upgrade.
entity-stuck-dying: An entity stranded in Dying has almost always tripped over a teardown hook that never returns success -- usually relation-departed, relation-broken, or stop crashing on a missing precondition the install path glossed over. The fix is charm-side: walk the teardown path in the failing hook with 'juju debug-log --include <unit>' open, make every cleanup step tolerant of partial state, then 'juju resolve' to retry. Reach for '--force' only as a last resort; it can leave substrate resources (volumes, security groups, K8s PVCs) orphaned outside Juju's view.
hook-error: An uncaught exception in a hook interrupts the §4c.1 firing protocol and forces the unit into error status, halting all subsequent reconciliation until an operator runs 'juju resolve'. The recovery is operator-side but the underlying defect is charm-side: locate the failing hook with 'juju debug-log --include <unit>' and 'juju show-status-log --type=juju-unit <unit>', fix the exception path in charm code, then push a new revision. A unit that errors silently and recovers via auto-resolve will keep erroring on the same condition.
model-suspended-credential: A suspended model silently swallows every workload command -- 'juju deploy', 'juju refresh', 'juju add-unit' will all hang indefinitely until the cloud credential is restored. The trigger is almost always credential rotation outside Juju's view: an expired service principal, a revoked access key, or a deleted IAM role. Refresh the credential via 'juju add-credential <cloud>' followed by 'juju update-credential', and confirm the cloud admin has not invalidated the principal. '--force' does NOT bypass model suspension.
status-churn: A churning workload status undermines the contract that status reads can be trusted. Every consumer of juju status (operators, integration tests, COS dashboards) is reading stale data the moment they look. Audit collect_unit_status across consecutive reconciles: identify which precondition oscillates, eliminate the race between handlers, or merge near-identical states into a single canonical one. Stability of declared state is the §4c.2 protocol's core promise.
stuck-maintenance: Maintenance is for long-running but bounded work. A unit that has held maintenance status without transitioning has either silently failed its long task, never sets a terminal status, or is using maintenance as a stand-in for waiting/active. The longer this lasts, the more operators tune out the maintenance signal entirely. Audit the reconciler: identify the path that should mark the work complete and transition out of maintenance, or reconsider whether this state should have been waiting (external dependency) or active (idle) all along.
unit-blocked-stale: Blocked status means the charm is waiting for human intervention. The longer a unit sits blocked, the higher the risk of cascading failures, drift between expectations and reality, and erosion of trust in operator tooling. Read the workload status message, address the cited cause, then nudge the charm or wait for the next event.
